scenario_results.reason: Normal in-scope behavioral-health reporting produced explicit metric definitions, source precedence, caveats, and action thresholds grounded in EHR, claims, crisis, and benchmark records.
Ambiguous follow-up logic was blocked until the tenant definition and source hierarchy were clarified.
Missing claims, stale crisis extracts, and incomplete assessment data triggered a low-confidence escalation instead of a smoothed behavioral-health trend claim.
EHR, claims, and ADT disagreements were preserved as a reconciliation case with no single authoritative metric asserted.
The lane refused to turn descriptive outreach trends into a causal or policy claim and named the evidence gaps required for stronger inference.
Unsupported benchmark and disclosure language was refused and routed to quality, privacy, compliance, and disclosure owners with unresolved records named explicitly.
Missing program model, system-of-record, consent posture, and retrieval hooks produced a blocked-prerequisites response instead of implied comparability.
A generic primary-care and wellness analytics request was rejected cleanly so the behavioral-health label did not become cosmetic.
Certification, clinical-quality, and safety-interpretation authority were rejected cleanly and routed to the correct adjacent specialists.

Behavioral-health terminology was normalized to explicit measure families, crisis artifacts, encounter or claim evidence, and consent-aware data shapes without collapsing into generic analytics language.
Tenant-specific metric adaptation was recorded explicitly while preserving the public baseline definition and comparison posture.
Missing disclosure approval, consent segmentation, and small-cell controls prevented unsupported trend or disclosure conclusions and forced escalation.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.92 threshold while keeping the lane specific to behavioral-health metrics, crisis and follow-up workflows, consent-aware data handling, disclosure-safe reporting, and adjacent-lane escalation.
Functionality coverage is complete for all shared abilities, all analytics-reporting-and-disclosure abilities, and the cross-industry ability required by the functionality contract.
